text: Hello, this is a forty-year-old female who had a laparoscopic gastric bypass ten years ago, who presented with a few days of crampy abdominal pain, who was found to have an interception at the JJ. On initial entrance into the abdomen, there was no dilated small bowel limbs and no signs of ischemia. The JJ was easily found and we began to run the limbs. The Roux limb was retrocholic, retrogastric and was fairly short, but no signs of obstruction. And then we ran the biliopancreatic limb and no obstruction to the ligament trites.

The surgeon evaluates the gastric pouch, Peterson's defect, and JJ, confirming no internal hernias. The common channel is examined from the JJ to the terminal ileum, showing no issues. Laxity is observed in the common channel, with contrast visible moving retrograde into the JJ and up to the biliopancreatic limb.

The surgeon performs a plication of the common channel to the biliopancreatic limb at the site of observed laxity, using two permanent sutures to align the tissues and prevent kinks, aiming to prevent further intussusception and alleviate symptoms.

The patient is one month out from surgery with no recurrence of her severe symptoms. I'm interested to see who would do a placation in this patient or simply let it be since it's self-reduced or who would actually perform a JJ resection on this patient. Thank you for watching and I'll look forward to the comments.
